KBG Syndrome

Last Update: October 12, 2023

Summary

KBG syndrome is a condition caused by pathogenic variants in ANKRD11. Clinical features include intellectual disability, characteristic facial features, and skeletal anomalies. Inheritance is autosomal dominant.

Diagnosis

Suggestive Findings

The diagnosis should be considered in individuals with macrodontia of the upper central incisors, short stature, and developmental delay.

Establishing the Diagnosis

The diagnosis is established by identification of a heterozygous pathogenic variant in ANKRD11 on molecular genetic testing.

Clinical Description

Intellectual disability is present in the majority of affected individuals and is typically mild to moderate. Short stature is observed in approximately 70% of affected individuals. Seizures occur in around 20% of reported cases. Hearing impairment is not observed in this syndrome.

Genotype-Phenotype Correlations

No clear genotype-phenotype correlation has been established for KBG syndrome.

Management

Treatment of Manifestations

Seizures are typically treated with standard antiepileptic medications. Speech therapy and occupational therapy are recommended for affected children.

Surveillance

Annual audiologic evaluation and developmental assessment are recommended through adolescence.

Agents/Circumstances to Avoid

No specific agents or circumstances need to be avoided in KBG syndrome.

Therapies Under Investigation

No targeted therapies are currently under investigation for KBG syndrome.

Genetic Counseling

KBG syndrome is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner. Approximately 70% of cases are de novo.

Molecular Genetics

ANKRD11 functions as a chromatin regulator. Pathogenic variants typically act through haploinsufficiency.

Pharmacogenomic Considerations

This is a deliberately-unmatched heading to exercise the overflow lane.

References

Reference list omitted in fixture.
